coredata.dc:title: Amplified fluorescence detection and adsorption of Au3+ by the fluorescent melamine formaldehyde microspheres incorporated with N and S co-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Si
Yu, Xu
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Gold is one of the potential toxic heavy metals. In the present study, Au3+ was detected and removed by newly-designed fluorescent microspheres (MF-CDs), i.e. melamine formaldehyde microspheres incorporated with N and S co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs). N,S-CDs played the role as sensing unites and melamine formaldehyde microspheres (MF) as carriers. When MF-CDs were attempted as the fluorescence probe, enhanced fluorescence sensing performance towards Au3+ was achieved with wider linear range (0.05–2 μM) and lower limit of detection (31 nM) compared to the N,S-CDs probe. In addition, when MF-CDs were used as the adsorbent, the adsorption capacity towards Au3+ reached up to 1 mmol g−1, about ten times more than that of MF. Moreover, the Au3+ adsorbed on the MF-CDs could be in-situ transferred to gold nanoparticle (AuNP), forming the immobilized nanocatalyst, i.e. MF-CDs-AuNP, which could further assist the reduction of 4-nitrophenol with acceptable reusability. This study paved an avenue to design the multifunctional materials for simultaneous detection, removal and recycling of environmental concerned pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Melamine formaldehyde microspheres
Au3+
Fluorescence sensor
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Au3+ by MF-CDs
The reduction of the adsorbed Au3+ to AuNP and the catalytic performance of AuNP
The amplified detection of Au3+ with MF-CDs
The catalytic activity and reusability of MF-CDs-AuNP for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2172019KFYRCPY112). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for instrumental support.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2172019KFYRCPY112 ). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for instrumental support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of N,S-CDs; HRTEM image and size distribution of N,S-CDs (inset); (b) the SEM image of MF-CDs; (c) the fluorescence microscope image of MF-CDs and MF (inset).
The FT-IR spectra of N,S-CDs, MF-CDs and MF.
Full scan XPS spectra (a) and high resolution spectra (b-e) of MF-CDs (b–d).

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum and the fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs under different excitation wavelengths of 300–370 nm (inset); (b) the UV–vis absorption spectrum and the fluorescence spectra of MF-CDs under different excitation wavelengths of 300–370 nm (inset); the time (c) and pH stability (d) of MF-CDs.

(a) The effect of pH on the detection of Au3+; (b) the detection kinetics under pH 2 and 10 μM Au3+; (c) the fluorescence spectra of MF-CDs upon addition of Au3+ from 0 μM to 10 μM; (d) the relationship between F/F0 and concentration of Au3+ from 0.05 to 2 μM; (e) the Stern–Volmer linear fitting curve of MF-CDs toward Au3+ from 0.05 to 10 μΜ; (f) the selectivity of MF-CDs over other ions and their co-existing interference; (g) the relationship between F/F0 and concentration of Au3+ from 0.1 μM to 2 μM in the Yangtze River.
(a) The effect of pH on the adsorption of Au3+ onto MF-CDs and MF; (b) the adsorption kinetics of Au3+ onto MF-CDs and MF; (c) the adsorption isotherm of Au3+ onto MF-CDs and MF.

(a) UV-Vis absorption spectra during the catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol by MF-CDs-AuNP; (b) the linear relationship between ln(A/A0) and reaction time for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol by MF-CDs-AuNP and MF-AuNP.
The comparison towards the detection of Au3+ between MF-CDs and other reported materials.
The kinetic and isotherm models of MF-CDs and MF towards Au3+ adsorption.
The comparison towards the adsorption of Au3+ between MF-CDs and other reported adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Amplified fluorescence detection and adsorption of Au3+ by the fluorescent melamine formaldehyde microspheres incorporated with N and S co-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gold is one of the potential toxic heavy metals. In the present study, Au3+ was detected and removed by newly-designed fluorescent microspheres (MF-CDs), i.e. melamine formaldehyde microspheres incorporated with N and S co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs). N,S-CDs played the role as sensing unites and melamine formaldehyde microspheres (MF) as carriers. When MF-CDs were attempted as the fluorescence probe, enhanced fluorescence sensing performance towards Au3+ was achieved with wider linear range (0.05–2 μM) and lower limit of detection (31 nM) compared to the N,S-CDs probe. In addition, when MF-CDs were used as the adsorbent, the adsorption capacity towards Au3+ reached up to 1 mmol g−1, about ten times more than that of MF. Moreover, the Au3+ adsorbed on the MF-CDs could be in-situ transferred to gold nanoparticle (AuNP), forming the immobilized nanocatalyst, i.e. MF-CDs-AuNP, which could further assist the reduction of 4-nitrophenol with acceptable reusability. This study paved an avenue to design the multifunctional materials for simultaneous detection, removal and recycling of environmental concerned pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: As the precious metal, gold has been widely applied in industry, clinical therapy, research and so on (Hashmi and Hutchings, 2006; Yeh et al., 2012; Bertrand et al., 2018; Xiao et al., 2020). In despite of great value in practice, gold was claimed to have severe toxicity. Taking Au3+ as an example, it caused damage to the liver, kidney and nervous system by binding to DNA and enzymes (Kambam et al., 2015). With the increasing usage of Au3+, delivery of Au3+ to the environment becomes ubiquitous, thus leading to the accelerated exposure to humans and organisms. Hence, it is crucial to monitor and remove Au3+ in the environment for sustainable development.

For the determination of residual Au3+, instrumental technologies, e.g. atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and atomic force microscope, played predominant roles. Inevitably, these technologies normally required the tedious sample pretreatment, expensive instrumental invest and skilled operators. Recently, thanks to the rapid expansion of fluorescence sensors merited with simple and fast operation, they emerged as alternative methods to address these problems (Lia et al., 2016; Ramanan et al., 2018; Rahmani and Ghaemy, 2019; Sharma et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2018). Moreover, for the removal of Au3+, adsorption was a preferred method characteristic of simplicity, reusability and no other transformation products; diverse adsorbents were explored for this purpose (Ertan and Gülfen, 2009; Aydın et al., 2008; Fujiwara et al., 2007; Abd Razak et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2010; Feng et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017b). However, the majority of the reported methods focused on the single aim, either determination or adsorption of Au3+, especially less on the recycling of gold after detection or adsorption. For the consideration of sustainability and economy, there is a great demand to develop all-in-one materials in combination of detection, adsorption and recycling ability.

In the present study, a kind of fluorescent microspheres was designed with carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescence sensing units and melamine formaldehyde microspheres (MF) as carriers. MF with satisfactory thermal and solvent stabilities (Wu et al., 2013) was widely attempted as adsorbent (Ming et al., 2016), sensor substrates (You et al., 2013), catalytic carriers (Xing, 2013) and templates (Fu et al., 2013). MF is well known for the abundant N element, which is supposed to have high special affinity to Au3+ (Qin et al., 2019), and thus makes it a promising candidate for the capture of Au3+. Additionally, under acid catalysis and heating synthetic condition, the highly branched MF pre-polymer was capable to efficiently incorporate fluorophores (Wu et al., 2014), harvesting the stable fluorescent microspheres. Hence, MF was herein selected as the substrate. On the other hand, CDs are characteristic of high quantum yield, good solubility and biocompatibility, low toxicity and cost (Li et al., 2018a), and demonstrate their potential applicability as the fluorescence probe. In light of the special affinity for Au3+, N and S co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) were deliberately chosen as the fluorescence sensing units.

When N,S-CDs were incorporated into MF (MF-CDs), particularly, thanks to the amplification effect of the MF substrate and the collective effect of N,S-CDs on MF-CDs (Wang et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2017; Montalti et al., 2015; Tian et al., 2014), the much higher fluorescence detection sensitivity and wider linearity towards Au3+ could be expected using MF-CDs as the fluorescence probe. As well, MF-CDs exhibited favorable adsorption performance towards Au3+, as a result of the cooperative adsorption ability of N,S-CDs and MF. Furthermore, Au3+ adsorbed on MF-CDs could be in-situ transferred to gold nanoparticle (AuNP) with good catalytic property, realizing the reusability of precious metal. In a word, the proposed MF-CDs were versatile with excellent detection, adsorption and recycling performance.

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Chemicals, including melamine, paraformaldehyde, anhydrous citric acid, urea, thiourea, 4-nitrophenol, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, KNO3, ZrSO4·4H2O, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, AgNO3, NiSO4·6H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, MnSO4·H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, AlCl3, BaCl2, NaF, NaCl, Na2SO3, NaNO2, Na2SO4, Na2HPO4·6H2O, Na2CO3, NaHCO3 and NaAcO, were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Purified water was used throughout the experiment. All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification, except as stated otherwise.

A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) and a JSM-35CF scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, Japan) were used to observe the morphology of the materials. Fluorescence images were observed by DP73 fluorescence microscope (OLYMPUS, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was acquired on the XRD-6100 (Shimadzu, Japan) from 20° to 80° at 5° min−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were carried out on an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FT-IR) spectra were studied by an AVATAR 360 transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo, USA). All fluorescence spectra and intensity were obtained by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) with excitation and emission slit set as 5 nm. UV–vis measurement was carried out by a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). TAS-986 flame atomic absorption spectrometer (FAAS, Pu Xi, China) was applied to quantify the concentration of Au3+.

N,S-CDs were prepared according to the literature (Wang et al., 2018) with a little modification. Anhydrous citric acid (0.093 M), urea (0.42 M) and thiourea (0.14 M) were mixed in 10 mL pure water, and heated in a teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 185 °C for 6 h. The obtained aqueous solution was dialyzed against purified water through dialysis bag (Mw 2000) for 24 h with renewing water every 4 h to purify the products. Afterwards, the residual solution in the dialysis bag was then evaporated and dried at 80 °C to gather the N,S-CDs powder.

Melamine (2.6 g) and paraformaldehyde (3.7 g) were dissolved in water (50 mL) with stirring at 50 °C until forming the clear and transparent solution, which was the MF pre-polymer solution (Wu et al., 2014). N,S-CDs were dissolved in pure water to form homogenous solution as 2.6 mg mL−1. To evaluate the influence of ratio of N,S-CDs to MF pre-polymer on the fluorescence intensity of the final material, the N,S-CDs solution and MF pre-polymer solution was mixed at different volume ratios from 14:1–1:14 with the fixed volume of 15 mL; this solution was then mixed with 30 mL diluted hydrochloric acid (0.4 mM) with heating at 100 °C for 30 min. The yellow solid was produced, washed with abundant water, isolated by centrifugation and dried at 80 °C. Thus-obtained material was used for the fluorescence measurement, as depicted in Fig. S1. The volume ratio of N,S-CDs to MF pre-polymer solution at 3:2 resulted in the strongest fluorescence, and was thus chosen for the MF-CDs preparation. For comparison, MF was prepared with the same process but without the addition of N,S-CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Au3+ by MF-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, MF-CDs were dispersed in purified water and sonicated to form homogeneous suspension stock solution (2 mg mL−1). The MF-CDs solution of prescribed volume was incubated with the Au3+ solution for the prescribed time. Afterwards, the sample was subjected to fluorescence measurement with emission wavelength of 450 nm and excitation wavelength of 340 nm at room temperature. F/F0 was the criterion to evaluate the quenching efficiency, where F represented the fluorescence intensity of MF-CDs solution in the presence of Au3+ and F0 was the fluorescence intensity of initial MF-CDs solution without Au3+ addition.

To optimize detection pH, MF-CDs stock solution (80 μL) and Au3+ solution (10 μM, 1.92 mL) with the desired pH adjusted by HCl or NaOH were incubated at 40 rpm for 3 h and then subjected to the fluorescence measurement.

To test the detection selectivity of MF-CDs towards Au3+, some cations and anions solutions (1.92 mL, 30 μM) were separately incubated with MF-CDs stock solution (80 μL) at pH 2 and 40 rpm for 2 h, and then for the fluorescence measurement. Moreover, each selected possible interference ion (3 mM, 20 μL) was alone introduced to the mixture of MF-CDs stock solution (80 μL) and Au3+ solution (10 μM, 1.92 mL) at pH 2 and 40 rpm for 2 h to assess the co-existing interference. I/I0 was the criterion, where I represented the fluorescence intensity of MF-CDs solution with interference ions and/or Au3+, and I0 was the fluorescence intensity of initial MF-CDs solution.

For genuine sample analysis, the Yangtze River water (Wuhan, China), checked to be free of Au3+ by FAAS, was taken as an example. The Yangtze River water was firstly added with BaCl2 to precipitate possible SO4 2–, and filtered with 0.22 µm membrane. A series of Au3+ with specified concentration were spiked into the treated Yangtze River water. The Au3+ spiked river water was incubated with the MF-CDs stock solution (80 μL) of pH 2 under stirring at 40 rpm for 2 h, and then for the fluorescence measurement.

To optimize the adsorption pH of Au3+, MF-CDs (5 mg) were dispersed in the Au3+ aqueous solution (0.5 mM, 5 mL) with pH from 2 to 10 adjusted by HCl or NaOH. The adsorption was performed at 40 rpm and 20 °C for 12 h. The adsorption capacity of MF-CDs towards Au3+ was calculated by Eq. (I).
(I)
Q
e
=
C
0
−
C
e
V
m

Qe (mmol g−1) represented the equilibrium adsorption capacity; C0 and Ce (mM) were the initial and equilibrium concentrations of Au3+ in the supernatant, respectively; V (L) denoted the volume of adsorption system; m (g) was the weight of the adsorbent used.

Adsorption kinetics of Au3+ onto MF-CDs was studied as follows. MF-CDs (5 mg) were dispersed in Au3+ solution (0.5 mM, 5 mL, pH 2) accompanying with constant agitation at 40 rpm and 20 °C. The residual concentration of Au3+ in the supernatant was monitored by FAAS at prescribed interval time. Adsorption kinetic models, i.e. pseudo-first-order (Eq. (II)) and pseudo-second-order (Eq. (III)) (Feng et al., 2018), were used to simulate the adsorption process.
(II)

t

Adsorption isotherm was obtained as below. The adsorption of Au3+ ranging from 0.007 to 3 mM (pH 2, 5 mL) by MF-CDs (5 mg) was individually performed at 20 °C and 40 rpm for 4 h. The experimental data were fitted by Langmuir (Eq. (IV)) and Freundlich (Eq. (V)) models (Li et al., 2018b).
(IV)

f
Qmax was the maximum adsorption capacity; KL (L mmol−1) was the Langmuir constant; Kf [(mmmol g−1) (L mmmol−1)1/n was the Freundlich constant.

Dubinin–Radushkevich (D–R) isotherm model (Eq. (VI)) (Zheng et al., 2009) was introduced to test whether the adsorption was physical or chemical.
(VI)

Q

Q

2

Qe was the adsorption capacity at equilibrium (mmol g−1); Qm was the maximum monolayer adsorption capacity (mmol g−1); β (mol2 J−2) was adsorption energy constant, and ε was Polanyi potential obtained from Eq. (VII).
(VII)
ε = RT

)
R was the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1); T was the absolute temperature (K) and Ce was the equilibrium concentration of Au3+ (mol L−1).

E (kJ mol−1) represented the mean free energy, which was calculated by Eq. (VIII). A chemical adsorption was speculated (8 kJ mol−1 <E < 16 kJ mol−1), and a physical one was speculated (E < 8 kJ mol−1).
(VIII)
E =
1
2 β
For comparison, MF was directly used as the adsorbent for Au3+ adsorption as the same operation as above.

To study the adsorption selectivity of MF-CDs towards Au3+, each interference ion (0.5 mM), e.g. CO3 2–, SO4 2–, Cl–, Ag+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Fe3+, was individually spiked to the Au3+ (0.5 mM) aqueous solution of pH 2. The adsorption was performed at 20 °C and 40 rpm for 4 h. The adsorption capacity of MF-CDs towards Au3+ (Qe) was calculated by Eq. (I) to evaluate the selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The reduction of the adsorbed Au3+ to AuNP and the catalytic performance of AuNP
ce:section.ce:para: To transfer the adsorbed Au3+ to AuNP, MF-CDs-Au3+ (MF-CDs adsorbed Au3+ at equilibrium) of 0.1 mmol g−1 was dispersed to NaBH4 solution (0.1 M) with agitation until the dark red color appeared, indicating the transformation of Au3+ to AuNP. The solid, i.e. MF-CDs-AuNP, was washed with purified water and dried at 80 °C for further use.

For the catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol, MF-CDs-AuNP (10 mg) was dispersed to 50 mL aqueous solution containing 4-nitrophenol (0.025 mM) and NaBH4 (50 mM) with stirring. The UV-Vis spectra of the solution were monitored at prescribed interval time. The catalytic kinetics was modulated by the pseudo-first-order model (Eq. (IX)).
(IX)
ln
A
A
0
= − kt
A0 and A denoted the absorbance of 4-nitrophenol at the initial time and time t (min), respectively; k was the reaction rate constant.
For comparison, MF-Au3+ (MF adsorbed Au3+ at equilibrium) was also transferred to MF-AuNP, which was then tested as the catalyst for 4-nitrophenol reduction according to the above same procedure.

After every catalytic reaction, MF-CDs-AuNP was isolated by centrifugation, washed with water and dried for regeneration. The regenerated MF-CDs-AuNP was used again for the next cycle of catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol as the above procedure. The reusability of MF-CDs-AuNP was tested for five cycles.

In this study, the N,S-CDs sensitive to Au3+ were deliberately designed and prepared via hydrothermal method. From the TEM image in Fig. 1(a), the prepared N,S-CDs were spherical with the size of between 5.2 and 7.1 nm averaging at 6.2 nm. The lattice spacing was 0.34 nm, representing the plane (002) of graphene (Wang et al., 2017a). The prepared MF-CDs also exhibited uniform size distribution with the average size of 2.5 ± 0.5 µm (Fig. 1(b)). Additionally, the blue fluorescence of MF-CDs was observed (Fig. 1(c)) via the fluorescence microscope with the excitation wavelength at 405 nm, while no fluorescence was observed for MF (Fig. 1(c) inset).

The FT-IR and XPS were combined to analyze the chemical composition of N,S-CDs and MF-CDs. In Fig. 2 of FT-IR spectra, for N,S-CDs, the absorption at 3425 cm−1 (υOH/–NH), 1628 cm−1 (υ–C ˭O), 1578 cm−1 (υ–C ˭N), 1417 cm−1 (υ–O ˭C–N), 1186 cm−1 (υ–C ˭S), 1100 cm−1 (υ–C–O–) and 1000 cm−1 (υ–C–S) was obvious (Wang et al., 2018; Li et al., 2016). Comparing MF and MF-CDs, they shared the same absorption at 3384 cm−1 (υ–OH/–NH ), 1554 cm−1 and 816 cm−1(υ-1,3,5-triazine ring), 1497 cm−1 (σ–CH2), 1340 cm−1 (β–C–H), 1158 cm−1 (υ–C–N–) and 1009 cm−1 (υ–C–O–), which was characteristic of the MF functionalities (Li et al., 2017). However, in the case of MF-CDs, peaks at 1628 cm−1 (υ–C ˭O) and 1417 cm−1 (υ–O ˭C–N) for N,S-CDs disappeared, probably due to the reduction of –C˭O during the preparation of MF-CDs. In addition, peaks at 3425 cm−1 (υ–OH/–NH), 1578 cm−1 (υ–C ˭N) and 1000 cm−1 (υ–C–S) were blue shifted to 3384, 1554 and 974 cm−1, respectively, possibly because of the formation of the hydrogen bonds between N,S-CDs and MF (Ding et al., 2019). Moreover, the peak at 1186 cm−1 (υ–C ˭S) was red shifted to 1202 cm−1, possibly relative to the change of electron density around the –C˭S once N,S-CDs interacted with MF. Furthermore, as illustrated in XPS for MF-CDs ( Fig. 3(a)), four major peaks, i.e. 163.9, 287, 398.9 and 532.2 eV assigning to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, were observed. Respective high resolution spectra and assignment of binding energy of these peaks are depicted in Fig. 3(b)–(e) (Li et al., 2018a; Chen et al., 2016; Figueras et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). All the above characterizations disclosed the existence of functional groups in MF-CDs, e.g. C˭S, C˭N, C–S and C–N, demonstrating the successful immobilization of N,S-CDs onto MF.

In Fig. 4(a), for the pristine N,S-CDs, an obvious UV absorption peak at 340 nm was present, possibly attributed to the n-π* electron transition of C˭O or C˭N (Hasan et al., 2018). This UV absorption peak was a little red shifted to ~355 nm (Fig. 4(b)) after N,S-CDs were incorporated into MF, while absent in MF. In addition, both N,S-CDs (Fig. 4(a) inset) and MF-CDs (Fig. 4(b) inset) exhibited the excitation-independent fluorescence emission with the maximum emission at 443 and 451 nm, respectively. Several stages, e.g. doping, nucleation, aggregation and growth, may occur during the formation of MF-CDs. As a result, the CDs in MF-CDs may aggregate, leading to red-shift emission (Wu et al., 2019).
The fluorescence of MF-CDs was stable within 7 days (Fig. 4(c)) and pH 2–12 (Fig. 4(d)). This favorable stability of MF-CDs laid the foundation for the further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The amplified detection of Au3+ with MF-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: MF-CDs were expected as the fluorescence probe for Au3+ detection, since the N,S-CDs were sensitive to Au3+ and MF had the additional affinity for Au3+. To optimize the sensing conditions, sample pH and incubation time were investigated as depicted in Fig. 5(a) and (b). The lowest F/F0 values were obtained at sample pH of 2 and incubation time of 2 h, which were the optimum conditions and adopted in the subsequent experiments. However, when the pristine N,S-CDs were used as the probe for Au3+, pH 5 and 30 min could achieve the optimal detection (Fig. S2 (a) and (b)). The different optimal sensing conditions between MF-CDs and N,S-CDs may be related to the MF substrate. The lower pH was beneficial to the adsorption of Au3+ onto the MF (as discussed in Section 3.3), thus leading to the enhanced quenching effect. Anyway, although pH 5 was the most sensitive pH in the case of N,S-CDs, the variation of the sensing signal at pH 2–6 was not that significant. The incubation time with MF-CDs was a little longer, as it may take more time for Au3+ to reach adsorption equilibrium with MF-CDs.

The fluorescence of MF-CDs was weakened with the increasing Au3+ concentration, as depicted in Fig. 5(c). In Fig. 5(d), the values of F/F0 were in a good linearity with the concentration of Au3+ (0.05~2 μM) with correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9950. The detection limit (LOD) reached as low as 31 nM. Hence, MF-CDs were suitable as the fluorescence probe for Au3+ quantification. As a comparison, using the N,S-CDs as the probe for Au3+, under the optimum detection condition, the linearity from 1~10 μM with R2 of 0.9981 and an LOD of 842 nM were obtained (Fig. S2 (c) and (d)). Obviously, the MF-CDs probe provided wider linear range and lower LOD. Also, compared to the other reported CDs in Table 1, MF-CDs showed satisfactory detection ability towards Au3+.

The reasons for the above results could be explained as below. The fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs by Au3+ occurred due to the non-radiative electron-transfer from N,S-CDs to Au3+ on the basis of the affinity between Au and N/S (Rahmani and Ghaemy, 2019). The excellent fluorescence sensing ability of MF-CDs could be due to the amplification effect of the MF substrate and the collective effect of N,S-CDs on MF-CDs (Wang et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2017; Montalti et al., 2015; Tian et al., 2014). Specifically, in acidic condition, Au3+ predominantly existed as AuClxOH(4−x) in the solution while MF matrix was protonated, promoting the electrostatic binding between them (Huang et al., 2010), in addition to the special affinity of Au with N (Qin et al., 2019). As a result, Au3+ was captured and enriched on the MF with enhanced local concentration around the N,S-CDs on MF-CDs (Wang et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2017). Additionally, N,S-CDs of high density were present on MF-CDs and more N,S-CDs were available to the Au3+ than the pristine N,S-CDs. Thus, one single Au3+ might interact with multiple N,S-CDs and the cooperative process could further strengthen the fluorescence quenching (Montalti et al., 2015; Tian et al., 2014). However, possibly owing to adsorption of Au3+ onto the MF and more N,S-CDs were effective for the detection, longer incubation time was required in the case of MF-CDs as the fluorescence probe.

The fluorescence quenching efficiency could be determined by the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. X) (Tian et al., 2014).
(X)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
C
A
u
3 +

F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of the probe in the absence and presence of the target analyte (Au3+), respectively; CAu 3+ was the concentration of Au3+; KSV denoted the quenching efficiency constant.

From Fig. 5(e) and Fig. S2(e), the KSV was 0.49 μM−1 for MF-CDs in the case of CAu 3+ from 0.05 to 2 μM, while the KSV was 0.036 μM−1 for N,S-CDs when CAu 3+ was 1 to 10 μM. The much larger KSV for MF-CDs implied the higher sensitivity to Au3+ than the pristine N,S-CDs (Zhou et al., 2017).

To test the selectivity of MF-CDs probe, all the individual suspect interference ions were introduced up to three times concentration as much as Au3+ to guarantee the reliability of test results, as depicted in Fig. 5(f). Only Ag+ and Hg2+ could quench the fluorescence of MF-CDs to some degree, relating to their affinity to MF-CDs. However, for the co-existing interference test, Hg2+ exhibited minor suppression effect while Ag+ exhibited none. This observation may be explained by the higher affinity of MF-CDs for Hg2+ than Ag+. Additionally, Ni2+ and SO4 2– exerted slight suppression effect. Hg2+, Ni2+ and SO4 2– may occupy some interactive sites on MF-CDs especially at high concentrations, and thus reduce binding sites for Au3+, leading to the weakened fluorescence quenching of MF-CDs by Au3+. Anyway, the affinity for Au3+ by MF-CDs was much higher than other studied ions, endowing MF-CDs acceptable specificity for Au3+.

The MF-CDs were applied to detect Au3+ in actual sample, taking the Yangtze River water as an example. As presented in Fig. 5(g), the detection linearity ranged from 0.1 to 2 μM with an LOD of 52 nM. Although matrix effect was obvious for the Yangtze River water, MF-CDs still possessed the high potential in the assay of Au3+ in the environmental water using the matrix-match detection.

Besides the fluorescence probe, MF-CDs were also attempted as the adsorbent towards Au3+ for removing it, using MF as the compared one. As depicted in Fig. 6(a), pH 2 was the optimum for both compared adsorbents, which agreed with the optimum detection pH of MF-CDs as discussed in Section 3.2.

In the case of the same adsorption condition, the equilibrium time for MF-CDs and MF was 4 and 6 h, respectively (Fig. 6(b)). As summarized in Table 2 and Fig. S3, for MF-CDs, pseudo-second-order better fitted the adsorption process with the higher R2 than that of pseudo-first-order, demonstrating that the chemical adsorption was largely involved in the adsorption. On the contrary, pseudo-first-order was more suitable for MF.

The adsorption isotherm is displayed in Fig. 6(c). The Langmuir and Freundlich models were used to model the isotherms, as summarized in Table 2 and Fig. S4. The R2 values of Langmuir isotherm model were higher than 0.999 for both MF-CDs and MF, disclosing the monolayer chemical adsorption of Au3+ onto the surface of these two adsorbents (Li et al., 2017). In addition, based on D-R isotherm model, E for both MF and MF-CDs were between 8 and 16 KJ mol−1, further indicating chemical adsorption process. According to the Langmuir isotherm model, the maximum monolayer adsorption capacity (Qmax) of MF-CDs was 1 mmol g−1, which was almost ten times as much as MF (0.12 mmol g−1). Compared with other reported adsorbents for Au3+ in Table 3, the adsorption capacity of MF-CDs was in the medium level. Anyway, thanks to the presence of N,S-CDs, MF-CDs owned more adsorption sites for the Au3+ than the corresponding substrate MF, leading to the enhanced adsorption capacity, demonstrating the role of N,S-CDs to assist the adsorption of Au3+. To further enhance the adsorption capacity, porous MF with large surface area could be a possible alternative substrate.

Additionally, from Fig. S5, the adsorption of Au3+ was a little suppressed by Hg2+ while other studied interference ions had no obvious effect. The satisfactory adsorption selectivity endowed MF-CDs great potential to be suitable adsorbent candidate for the removal of Au3+ from environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The catalytic activity and reusability of MF-CDs-AuNP for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: The reutilization of precious metal was a hotpot, which was conducive to sustainable development and economic viability. In our experiment, Au3+ on the MF-CDs and MF were in-situ converted to AuNP by the reductant of NaBH4 (Fig. S6). The diffraction peaks at the diffraction angles of 38.2°, 44.4°, 64.8° and 77.9° were observed for both MF-CDs-AuNP and MF-AuNP, which were ascribed to Au (111), (200), (220) and (311) diffraction (JCPDS card 04–0784) respectively, distinctly demonstrating the reduction of Au3+ to AuNP. MF-CDs-AuNP was herein attempted as the immobilized nanocatalyst, to catalyze the degradation of one pollutant, 4-nitrophenol, to 4-aminophenol with NaBH4 as the reductant. During this catalytic process, NaBH4 was hydrolyzed to B(OH)4- and active hydrogen, which was adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs and then reduced 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol (Zhao et al., 2015). In Fig. 7(a), as the reaction going on, the UV absorption peak at 400 nm derived from ionized 4-nitrophenol in alkaline condition became weakened, while a new peak at 300 nm representing 4-aminophenol appeared and strengthened (Deraedt et al., 2014). The catalytic process fitted the pseudo-first-order model with the rate constant (k) of 0.1442 min−1, while the rate constant for MF-AuNP was only 0.0705 min−1 (Fig. 7(b)). The N,S-CDs on MF-CDs may assist immobilized AuNP for catalysis, resulting in the higher catalytic activities.

Furthermore, the reusability performance of MF-CDs-AuNP was studied. The elimination percentage of 4-nitrophenol maintained as 93.65 ± 2.87% in five repeated usages, which was a good sign that the MF-CDs-AuNP could remain relatively stable catalytic ability, conforming to the green, economic and sustainable concepts.

A kind of versatile fluorescent microspheres, i.e. melamine formaldehyde microspheres incorporated with N and S co-doped carbon dots (MF-CDs), was prepared by the MF pre-polymer incorporating process. Due to the collective effect of N,S-CDs and enrichment of Au3+ on the MF-CDs, MF-CDs showed greater excellent sensing ability to Au3+ than pristine N,S-CDs with wider linear range and higher sensitivity. Moreover, MF-CDs owned satisfactory adsorption capacity towards Au3+. Last but not least, the adsorbed Au3+ was in-situ transferred to AuNP, which was then applied as efficient nanocatalyst to reduce 4-nitrophenol with stable catalytic performance. Our study set a good example to use the multifunctional materials for determination, removal and recycling of environmental pollutants.
Si Qin: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xu Yu: Revise Draft. Li Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2172019KFYRCPY112). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for instrumental support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.123978.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
